coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots originated from methyl red with molecular state and surface state controlled emissions for sensing and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Xiaohua
Zhang, Yan
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Yu, Guifeng
Zhao, Qiangqiang
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this article, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using methyl red (MR) as precursor in the absence and presence of ethanediamine (EDA) by facile hydrothermal pyrolysis, referring to as MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs, respectively. MR-CDs synthesized at different temperatures exhibited molecular state controlled photoluminescence (PL) property with the same characteristics (emission position and shape, no contribution from carbon core) as MR, except that their quantum yields (QYs) decreased with the reaction temperature increasing because of the consumption of MR. On the other hand, MR-EDA-CDs were fractionated into four fractions, which individually displayed unique surface state controlled PL property. Moreover, four fractions isolated from MR-EDA-CDs have specific surface groups that can selectively recognize pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively, in aqueous solution, then were developed as the corresponding sensors. In addition, MR-EDA-CDs are potential fluorescent agents with excellent biocompatibility for bioimaging of fungal cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecular state
Surface state
Sensing
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detections of multi-metal ions and pH values
Detections of multi-metal ions and pH values in real samples
Cytotoxicity of MR-EDA-CDs to the fungus
pH and Fe3+ sensors based on P-CDs
Detections of pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples
Bioimaging of fungal cells using B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs as label agents

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181 ), Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province , China (Nos. 2016GSF116007 , 2018GNC110006 ). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (No. ZR2014CM012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Emission spectra of MR-CDs (140-CDs, 160-CDs, 180-CDs and 200-CDs) and MR (λex = 300 nm, the concentrations of MR-CDs and MR were 0.1 mg/mL); (B) QYs of these prepared MR-CDs and MR (QYs were determined at absorbance values of around 0.081 and quinine sulfate with literature QY of 54.6% as the standard).
UV-visible absorption spectra of MR, MR-CDs (140-CDs, 160-CDs, 180-CDs and 200-CDs) and MR-EDA-CDs (P-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs, the absorbance values of all CDs is controlled below 1.0).

(A) Photographs of MR-EDA-CDs (P-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs, the concentration of MR-EDA-CDs is 0.1 mg/mL) in aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (B) Corresponding emission spectra of MR-EDA-CDs (P-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs) with maxima at 397, 470, 511 and 530 nm.
TEM images of MR-CDs ((A) 140-CDs, (B) 160-CDs, (C) 180-CDs and (D) 200-CDs) and MR-EDA-CDs ((E) P-CDs, (F) B-CDs, (G) G-CDs and (H) Y-CDs).
FTIR spectra of MR, MR-CDs (140-CDs, 160-CDs, 180-CDs and 200-CDs) and MR-EDA-CDs (P-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs).

A) PL emission spectra of P-CDs in 0.1 M phosphate-buffered solutions at various pH values. Insert: a linear correlation of PL intensity versus pH values over the range from 4.0 to 8.0. And emission spectra of B) P-CDs, C) B-CDs and D) G-CDs solutions at various concentrations of Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, excitation at 300, 320, 400 nm, respectively. The inserts: the linear relationships between 1 − P/P0 and the concentrations of Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of C. gloeosporioides fungus labelled by B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs.
Schematic illustrations of MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs synthese and multi functions of MR-EDA-CDs.
Analytical results for the detection of Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+ and pH in tap water and lake water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Results were expressed as the mean of three determinations ± standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) was defined as (SD/mean) × 100%.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots originated from methyl red with molecular state and surface state controlled emissions for sensing and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using methyl red (MR) as precursor in the absence and presence of ethanediamine (EDA) by facile hydrothermal pyrolysis, referring to as MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs, respectively. MR-CDs synthesized at different temperatures exhibited molecular state controlled photoluminescence (PL) property with the same characteristics (emission position and shape, no contribution from carbon core) as MR, except that their quantum yields (QYs) decreased with the reaction temperature increasing because of the consumption of MR. On the other hand, MR-EDA-CDs were fractionated into four fractions, which individually displayed unique surface state controlled PL property. Moreover, four fractions isolated from MR-EDA-CDs have specific surface groups that can selectively recognize pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively, in aqueous solution, then were developed as the corresponding sensors. In addition, MR-EDA-CDs are potential fluorescent agents with excellent biocompatibility for bioimaging of fungal cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a class of newly emerging carbon based nanomaterials, have been proven to possess high performances, including intense fluorescence [1], excellent photostability [2], favourable biocompatibility [3,4], low toxicity [5], outstanding water solubility [6], tunable fluorescence emission and excitation [7,8]. Due to their high performances, great potential for applications in wide areas, such as bioimaging [9,10], biosensing [11], medical diagnosis [12], drug delivery [13,14], catalysis [15] and photovoltaic devices [16,17] has attracted much more attention in scientific community. So it is very interesting to pursue more CDs with unique properties via function design so as to meet specific requirements, which need photoluminescence (PL) mechanism to be understood clearly.

CDs are generally believed to be composed of carbon-core and surface domains, which are characterized by graphitic-like sp2 carbon structure and abundant functional groups, respectively, and mainly responsible for their PL characteristics [6,18]. However, till now, their PL mechanism is still not very clear [19,20] because different precursors, even the same precursors under different conditions, will result in CDs with their own unique PL properties and ambiguous structures [21]. On the other hand, ideally simplified theoretical models presented in the literatures might ignore some complexity of their real composites [22–24]. Even so, PL mechanism in different manners such as quantum confinement effect [25], surface states [26], and molecular state [27,28] is well proposed and popularly accepted by scientists. Surface state as PL center is formed by the synergetic hybridization of chemical groups and carbon core, whereas molecule state as PL center is produced solely by an organic fluorophore [29,30].

Functional groups on the surface domain not only result in a series of emissive traps but also impart CDs with specific functions [31]. For example, CDs with surface functional groups as recognition moieties can selectively detect metal ions, such as Hg2+ [32], Cu2+ [33], Fe3+ [34], Pb2+ [35], Cr(VI) [36] and Ag+ [37], via forming non-fluorescent complexes. Moreover, surface modification is an effective strategy to further functionalize CDs according to the specific requirements in practical applications [9]. Guided by specific recognition molecules immobilized on their surfaces, CDs are endowed with extended functions in bioimaging at high resolution [9,10,38], accurate medical diagnosis and therapy [12,39], and controlled labelling for biological tracing [40]. Obviously, the functions of CDs have close relationship with surface domain.

In this work, methyl red (MR), 2-(4-dimethylaminophenylazo)benzoic acid, was expected to be a precursor for synthesizing CDs with new special functions by a facile hydrothermal pyrolysis method. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was, further, applied as a co-reactant to tune their structures and optical properties. Interestingly, the CDs synthesized with pure MR as precursor at different temperatures (MR-CDs) exhibited molecular state controlled emission. Whereas, the CDs prepared by MR in the presence of EDA (MR-EDA-CDs) could be fractionated into four fractions, which individually emitted unique surface state controlled photoluminescence. Moreover, different surface functional groups imparted MR-EDA-CDs with multi functions, such as pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ sensing, and bioimaging of fungal cells (see Scheme 1).

AgNO3 (≥ 99.8%), CaCl2 (≥ 99.0%), NiCl2 (≥ 98.0%), CoCl2 (≥ 99.0%), CdCl2 (≥ 98.0%), CuCl2 (≥ 99.0%), Fe(NO3)3 (≥ 98.5%), Hg(Ac)2 (≥ 98.0%), MgCl2 (≥ 99.0%), MnSO4 (≥ 99.0%), Pb(NO3)2 (≥ 99.0%), AlCl3 (≥99.0%), BaCl2 (≥ 99.0%), CrCl3 (≥ 99.0%), NaCl (≥ 99.5%), LiNO3 (≥ 99.5%), KCl (≥ 99.5%), NaOH (≥ 96.0%) and Zn(NO3)2 (≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Damao Chemical Corp (Tianjin, China). MR (≥ 99.0%), EDA (≥ 99.0%), NaH2PO4 (≥ 99.0%), Na2HPO4 (≥ 99.0%) and HCl (36 – 38%) were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used in this work are analytical reagents without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3 MΩ cm). The lake water samples were collected from Hongzi lake of Qingdao Agricultural University (located at 36.3°N and 120.4°E, water temperature of 19 °C). And the lake water were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.45 µm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. Tap water and lake water were used as the real samples. Generally, tap water contains a lot of cations and anions [41]. Besides cations and anions, there are many organic matters present in lake water [42].

The morphology and the size of CDs were analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7000, operated at 80 kV). PL spectra were performed by a fluorescence spectrometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CDs were collected using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min -1 in the range from 5° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of CDs were recorded using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with KBr pellets technique, over the range 400 – 4000 cm -1. The hydrodynamic sizes of CDs were measured via dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK) with standard parameters at 25 °C. The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared CDs were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excitation at optimum wavelength) and absorbance values at corresponding wavelength of the CDs aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfate [43].

MR-CDs were prepared through dissolving 0.2 g MR into 25 mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, MR solution was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave at 140, 160, 180 °C for 5 h and 200 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the deep red products were neutralized with 1 M NaOH solution. All the obtained materials were filtered through a 0.45 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. The solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500) for 2 days and concentrated by means of rotary evaporation at 60 °C. Finally, the solid CDs were obtained by freeze drying under vacuum at - 50 °C for 48 h.

MR-EDA-CDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of the mixture of MR and EDA. 0.2 g of MR and 1 mL of EDA were dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixture solution was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 5 h. Then the orange red products were neutralized with 1 M HCl solution. Finally, all the obtained materials were filtered through a 0.45 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. The solution was concentrated by means of rotary evaporation at 60 °C.

The components of MR-EDA-CDs were separated by column chromatography on a Sephadex G-25 gel filtration column using ultrapure water as eluent. MR-EDA-CDs aqueous solution was added to the gel column, and then eluted with ultrapure water. UV light at the wavelength of 365 nm was used to monitor the fractions eluted from the column. All solutions were concentrated by means of rotary evaporation at 60 °C. Finally, MR-EDA-CDs were obtained by freeze drying under vacuum at - 50 °C for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of multi-metal ions and pH values
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the assay solutions contained MR-EDA-CDs (P-CDs 0.05 mg/mL, B-CDs 0.1 mg/mL and G-CDs 0.02 mg/mL) and metal ions (Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+) at various concentrations in PB buffer (0.1 M, pH = 6.8), respectively. The PL spectra of the assay solutions were collected at room temperature. The signal output value was calculated according to the relative PL intensity 1 − P/P0, in which P0 and P represented PL intensities of MR-EDA-CDs at optimum wavelength in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. For the detection of pH values, P-CDs was added into PB buffer solutions (0.1 M) at various pH values. The method for the collection of PL spectra and the calculation of signal output values was the same as the detections of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of multi-metal ions and pH values in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the CDs based sensors for the detections of Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples, tap water and lake water were taken as real samples. The lake water samples were obtained from Hongzi Lake in the campus at Qingdao Agricultural University and the tap water samples from our lab. The tap water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively. And the lake water samples were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.45 µm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. The resultant lake water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively. Also tap and lake water samples were used as the real samples to evaluate the feasibility for the detection of pH values using P-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of MR-EDA-CDs to the fungus
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of MR-EDA-CDs to Colletotrichum gloeosporioides (C. gloeosporioides) fungus was assessed by the growth rate method. Mycelial disks (5 mm in diameter) of the test plant pathogenic fungus were placed in the center of sterile petri dishes (60 mm × 15 mm) which contained 5 mL potato dextrose agar (PDA) medium and the CDs at the concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 mg/mL. The plates were incubated at 27 ± 2 °C till the mycelia colonies of the control group reached the edge of the plates, and then mycelia growths with PDA containing the CDs were determined by measuring the colony diameter using decussating method. Each treatment was replicated 3 times. The survival percent (%) = A/A0 × 100% was calculated to assess the fungal viability in the presence of MR-EDA-CDs, where A0 was the mycelia growth diameter of the control group and A was the mycelia growth diameter of CDs treated group.

Mycelial disks were placed into liquid medium and incubated at 27 ± 2 °C for 3 days. The young vigorous mycelia were selected and washed 3 times with ultrapure water, then incubated in the PB buffer solution containing MR-EDA-CDs at a certain concentration for another 6 h in a constant temperature incubator. After washed 3 times with distilled water, the mycelia were imaged using a laser scanning confocal microscopy (Leica, Germany).

As shown in Fig. S1A, the precursor, MR, is a fluorescent agent with an excitation-independent emission located at 407 nm. Then MR-CDs were synthesized using pure MR as precursor by hydrothermal method at various temperatures of 140, 160, 180, 200 °C, referring to as 140-CDs, 160-CDs 180-CDs and 200-CDs, respectively. Their emission spectra were collected under excitations from 260 to 360 nm, which were identical to those of MR (such as the shape and the peak position, see Figs. 1A and S1). But the QYs of MR-CDs decreased with hydrothermal temperature increasing (Fig. 1B). Therefore, it can be assumed that the emissions of MR-CDs were contributed by the same fluorophore as MR, that is, the origin of the PL was attributed to the molecular state on the surface of the CDs. Moreover, as the fluorophore of MR was consumed with temperature increasing, a decrease was then followed in the quantum efficiency of MR-CDs. More evidence was supplied for the proposed PL origin by their absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 2, there are three typical absorption peaks at 300, 335 and 538 nm in the spectrum of MR. And they gradually became weak in the spectra of 140-CDs, 160-CDs 180-CDs and 200-CDs due to the consumption of MR on the surfaces as the hydrothermal temperature was enhanced (Fig. 2). When the reaction temperature reached 200 °C, there were no obvious absorptions in the spectrum of the CDs, suggesting the fluorophores on the surface of 200-CDs were largely carbonized (Fig. 2). Similar to MR, there are two bands in the excitation spectra monitored at different emission wavelengths for all MR-CDs (See Fig. S2), indicating that the optical bands were from MR molecules on their surface but not from the contribution of carbon core and surface state. Since protonation and deprotonation on the surface can give more information for emission mechanism of CDs [8], the effect of pH on the emissions of MR and MR-CDs was investigated by recording their spectra at different pH values. Under acidic condition, MR exhibited response to pH in the range of 3.0 – 5.0, and similar effect of pH was exerted on 140-CDs, 160-CDs and 180-CDs as shown in Fig. S3, which was another evidence to support that MR-CDs have the same fluorophore as MR [27]. But with reaction temperature increasing, the fluorophore on the dots was consumed and showed weaker response to pH under acidic condition. When most of the fluorophores on 200-CDs were consumed, an adverse pH effect was revealed. Moreover, the same types of metal ions showed quenching effects on the PL intensities of MR and MR-CDs (See Fig. S4), resulting from the same fluorophore as coordination sites.

In order to tune their emissions and composites, EDA was used as co-reactant to synthesize CDs with MR by a facile hydrothermal pyrolysis method (MR-EDA-CDs). Then, MR-EDA-CDs samples were purified on a Sephadex G-25 gel column, from which four fractions were obtained and emitted purple, blue, green and yellow PLs (emissions centered at round 398, 470, 511, and 530 nm, Fig. 3). Accordingly, they are referred to as P-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs in the following text. As can be seen from Fig S5, the emissions of the four fractions of MR-EDA-CDs exhibited excitation-independent properties, suggesting that the surface states on the four fractions were uniform. The QY of P-CDs is 54.1%, which is higher than those of MR and MR-CDs, suggesting the doping of EDA enhanced the quantum efficiency, while the QYs of B-, G- and Y-CDs are 15.9%, 8.6% and 1.7% respectively (Table S1). Therefore, the PL origins of MR-EDA-CDs were assigned to surface states and their characteristics were totally different with those of MR and MR-CDs. The surface state controlled mechanism can be confirmed by their excitation spectra monitored at different emission wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S6, P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs exhibited three bands in their excitation spectra at the entire monitoring emission wavelengths. The bands in the region of high energy might be from the sp2 domain of carbon core, and the bands at long wavelength were controlled by surface states. For P-, B- and G-CDs, the three bands in the excitation spectra exhibited independence on monitoring emission wavelength, that is to say, the electrons in different excited states relaxed to the uniform surface states by non-irradiation, and then the excited electrons at the uniform surface states were transited to ground states by irradiation, resulting in the excitation independent emission. However, there were several emission centers on the surface of Y-CDs since the three bands in the excitation spectra exhibited dependence on monitoring emission wavelengths (Fig. S6D). But the energy between the emission centers was slight different, leading to a nearly excitation-independent emission (Fig. S5D).

As shown in the absorption spectra (Fig. 2), P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs have absorption peaks at 243, 249, 234 and 255 nm in the high energy band, attributed to π→π* transitions in the π-conjugated structures [17]. And there are absorption peaks at 305, 405, 410 and 368 nm in the low energy band, which are assigned to n→π* transitions. Therefore, there were different sp2 domains in the carbon cores of P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs resulting in different electron donating centers [44], and different functional groups on the surfaces leading to different defect states.

In order to obtain more evidence for the surface state mechanism, the effect of pH on the PL of P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs was evaluated by collecting spectra in the solutions at different pH values (Fig. S7). For P-CDs, the PL intensity increased in the pH range from 3.0 to 8.0, and then became stable from 8.0 to 12.0. While B-CDs emitted much stronger PL under basic condition than under acidic condition. And the PL intensity changed slowly in the pH range from 3.0 to 5.0, but the intensity was kept stable in the pH range from 6.0 to 12.0. The PL intensity of G-CDs increased in the pH range from 3.0 to 7.0, and decreased from 11.0. Yet, Y-CDs was not too sensitive to pH variation. The emissions of P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs were influenced by pH to different degrees, attributed to the changes in the surface charges induced by protonation–deprotonation [8], which further suggests that their PL emission originated from different surface states.

The influences of metal ions on P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs were shown in Fig. S8. The PL of P-CDs responded to Fe3+, Ag+ and Pb2+, and the PL of B-CD could be quenched by Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+and Hg2+, among them was Ag+ exerted the most intense quenching effect. Fe3+, Al3+ and Hg2+ exhibited quenching effects on G-CDs, among them, Hg2+ was the most effective quencher. For Y-CDs, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ exerted slight quenching effects. Since the functional groups on the surface of MR-EDA-CDs have different abilities to coordinate with metal ion, P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs gave different quenching responses to metal ions, suggesting there were different surface states on the different fractions of MR-EDA-CDs.

The morphology and the size of MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs were well characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4A–D, all MR-CDs exhibited nearly spherical morphology. And the average sizes of the 140-CDs, 160-CDs, 180-CDs, and 200-CDs were 11.7 ± 2.0 nm, 10.4 ± 1.1 nm, 3.7 ± 1.0 nm and 16.1 ± 1.8 nm, measured by counting 100 particles in the images, respectively. Obviously, the size of the CDs decreased with the reaction temperature increasing, resulting from a more carbonization. While the reaction time reached 200 °C, the largest aggregation particles were obtained probably due to the quick carbonization of MR. As for MR-EDA-CDs, the average diameters of the P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs were 12.6 ± 2.4 nm, 5.5 ± 0.5 nm, 16.3 ± 2.9 nm and 4.5 ± 0.6 nm, respectively (Fig. 4E–H). The size order is G-CDs > P-CDs > B-CDs > Y-CDs, which suggests that the PL origins of MR-EDA-CDs were not from quantum confinement. Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameters were also determined by DLS to be 47.6, 40.0, 30.1 and 26.9 nm for P-, Y-, G- and B- CDs, respectively (Fig. S9). Nevertheless, their hydrodynamic diameters are obviously different from the dried sizes obtained by TEM characterization due to their hydration in solution. Dried sizes from TEM images illustrated their carbon core sizes because the water molecules attached on their surface were removed in dried state. However, the hydrodynamic sizes were contributed by the carbon core size and solvent shell thickness, which agrees with the column chromatography principle as the retention time increases with the hydrodynamic size of the CDs decreasing.

XRD was used to identify the crystalline of MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs. As shown in Fig. S10, there were no significant changes in XRD patterns of MR-CDs synthesized at different hydrothermal temperatures by comparison with MR. But the intensity of the peak at around 32.7°, which matched up with (002) facet of the carbon nitride (PDF 50-0664) [45], was enhanced with reaction temperature increasing, implying that MR gradually transformed into MR-CDs. In general, MR-CDs seems to have the similar crystalline with MR. However, obvious difference in XRD patterns of MR-EDA-CDs was observed by comparison with MR and MR-CDs. For example, new peaks at 25.7°, 26.7°, 27.5° emerged for P-CDs and G-CDs, and there were a broad peak at 22.9° with two shoulder peaks observed in the pattern of Y-CDs. Overall, compared with MR, the peaks at 32.7° disappeared in the XRD patterns of MR-EDA-CDs. Therefore, the crystalline is significantly different between the fractions of MR-EDA-CDs, and with that of MR and MR-CDs.

FT-IR was applied to characterize the functional groups on the surface of the CDs and MR. As shown in Fig. 5, the FTIR absorption spectra of MR-CDs are similar to that of pure MR. The characteristic absorption bands of pure MR were observed at 3479 cm -1 attributed to OH, 3126 – 2804 cm -1 to CH3, CH2 and CH, 1760 cm -1 to COOH, and 1602 cm -1 to N=N [46], which also can be found in the absorption spectra of MR-CDs (140-CDs, 160-CDs, 180-CDs and 200-CDs). Hence, the functional groups of MR were not destructed during the formation of MR-CDs via hydrothermal pyrolysis, though the absorptions of C–N (1387 cm -1) and C–O–C (around 1040 cm -1) in the spectra of MR-CDs became strong. The results confirm that the fluorophore from MR was maintained on the surface of MR-CDs. On the other hand, the FTIR spectra of the four fractions of MR-EDA-CDs are entirely different with each other, and with those of MR-CDs and pure MR. The absorption of OH could be observed in the spectra of all the four fractions. But the bands around 3000 cm -1 and 1602 cm -1 (N=N) were observed in the spectra of P-CDs and G-CDs, yet not in the spectra of B- and Y-CDs. The absorption of C=O exist in the spectra of P-, B- and Y-CDs and the absorption of N–H could be observed in P-CDs and G-CDs. And the absorptions in the range of 1500 – 1000 cm -1 were unique for the individual fractions. Therefore the four fractions have their own specific surface states.

MR-EDA-CDs are composed by four fractions (P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs), which possess their own unique surface states. As a result, P-, B-, G- and Y-CDs may respond to different metal ions and have potentials to be developed as the corresponding sensors. In general, the effects of metal ions on MR-EDA-CDs were evaluated by collecting their PL spectra of the dots in the presence of various metal ions (including Ag+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cr2+, Na+, Li+, K+ and Zn2+). And the sensitivity of MR-EDA-CDs to pH or metal ions was investigated by recording their PL spectra of the dots at different pH values or in the presence of metal ions at various concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and Fe3+ sensors based on P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the PL intensity of P-CDs increased gradually with pH values changing from 3.0 to 8.0, which suggests that P-CDs are sensitive to pH. A good linear relationship was revealed between the peak intensity at 397 nm and pH in the range of 4.0 – 8.0, and the regression equation is P = − 194.95 + 75.62 pH with the corresponding regression coefficient (R2) of 0.993. The PL intensity ratio P/P0 remained unchanged in a buffer solution after five cycles of pH switch between 3.8 and 8.0 (See Fig. S11). The reproducibility of the ratio of the emission intensity clearly demonstrates the excellent reversibility and the robustness of P-CDs.

Among all the tested metal ions, only Fe3+ significantly quenched while Ag+ and Pb2+ slightly affected the PL of P-CDs at the concentration of 200.0 μM (Fig. S8A). Also, P-CDs showed high sensitivity to Fe3+ since the PL intensity at 397 nm gradually decreased as the concentration of Fe3+ became higher (Fig. 6B). Two good linear correlations were obtained between the PL intensity ratio (1 − P/P0) and Fe3+ concentration in the ranges of 10.0 – 100.0 and 100.0 – 200.0 μM, respectively, (P0 and P are the PL intensities of P-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+) with the regression equations being 1 − P/P0 = 0.0070 + 0.0039 [Fe3+] (R2 = 0.998) and 1 − P/P0 = − 0.3597 + 0.0036 [Fe3+] (R2 = 0.994). The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed method is as low as 1.58 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3Sd/s).
Therefore, P-CDs has a potential to be developed as a nano sensor for the detections of pH and Fe3+.

B-CDs exhibited high selectivity and high sensitivity to Ag+ while the other tested metal ions (400.0 μM) exerted negligible effect on the dots (see Figs. S8B and 6C). The PL intensity ratio (1 − P/P0) showed a good linear relationship with Ag+ concentration in the range of 10.0 – 300.0 μM. The regression equation was calibrated as 1 − P/P0 = 0.0209 + 0.0014 [Ag+] (R2 = 0.994) with LOD being calculated to be 4.31 μM. Hence, B-CDs is a potential nano sensor for the detection of Ag+.

As can be seen from Figs. S8C and 6D, G-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity to Hg2+. A linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio (1 − P/P0) and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 2.0 – 20.0 μM was well calibrated as 1 − P/P0 = − 0.0019 + 0.0268 [Hg2+], with R2 being 0.996 and LOD as low as 0.40 μM. As a result, G-CDs is a candidate to be fabricated as a nano sensor for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the MR-EDA-CDs based sensors for the detections of pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples, recovery tests were performed in the tap water and lake water samples. The lake water was sampled from the Hongzi Lake in the campus of Qingdao Agricultural University and tap water from our lab. The tap water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively. After pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.45 µm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities, the lake water samples were also spiked with the different concentrations of Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively.

As presented in Table 1, the recoveries for the selective detection of Fe3+ in the tap water and lake water samples were in the ranges of 98.9 – 101.8% and 97.3 – 104.3%, and Ag+ in the tap water and lake water samples in the ranges of 98.1 – 101.1%, and 95.2 – 102.3%, and Hg2+ in the tap water and lake water samples in the ranges of 98.0 – 103.0% and 97.2 – 104.8%, respectively.

The detected results of pH in tap and lake water samples by using P-CDs based sensor were close to those obtained from conventional and commercial pH-meter. After three repeated determinations of pH in tap water and lake water samples by this sensor, the calculated values of RSD are all less than 5%.
The results indicated these sensors are of high feasibility, accuracy, stabilization and simplicity for the determinations of pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of fungal cells using B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs as label agents
ce:section.ce:para: Before bioimaging, the cytotoxicity of MR-EDA-CDs to C. gloeosporioides fungus was assessed by the growth rate method. Fungal mycelia growing on the PDA media containing B-, G- and Y-CDs at the concentrations of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mg/mL, respectively, showed no obvious difference on morphology and growth rate by comparison with the control (Fig. S12). Therefore, B-, G- and Y-CDs are excellent biocompatible and suitable for bioimaging of C. gloeosporioides.

The potential of MR-EDA-CDs as label agents in application for fluorescent imaging of pathogenic fungal cells (C. gloeosporioides) was investigated by means of a confocal microscopy. The fungal cells were isolated from liquid medium and washed several times using ultrapure water, and then cultured in the PB buffer solutions containing B-, G- and Y-CDs at the concentration of 1.5 mg/mL. Upon incubation at the room temperature for 6 h, the fungal cells were brightly illuminated when imaged on the confocal microscopy with excitation by 405 nm laser pulses, suggesting that the CDs were readily internalized into the fungal cells for imaging (see Fig. 7).

In this article, MR-CDs synthesized at different temperatures using pure MR as precursor exhibited emission property with the same characteristics (emission position and shape, no contribution from carbon core) as MR, except that the QYs decreased with the reaction temperature increasing because of the consumption of MR. On the other hand, MR-EDA-CDs were synthesized by MR in the presence of EDA, which could be fractionated into four fractions, emitting purple, blue, green and yellow PLs, respectively. Therefore, it can be deduced that the emissions of MR-CDs and MR-EDA-CDs were individually controlled by molecular state and surface state. Moreover, four fractions isolated from MR-EDA-CDs have specific surface groups that can selectively recognize pH, Fe3+, Ag+ and Hg2+, respectively, and can be developed as the corresponding sensors. In addition, MR-EDA-CDs are potential fluorescent agents with excellent biocompatibility for bioimaging of fungal cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province, China (Nos. 2016GSF116007, 2018GNC110006). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (No. ZR2014CM012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.08.007.
